extracted_information: The provided web content details Identity and Access Management (IAM) within cloud security, covering its definition, fundamental concepts, commonly used standards (authentication/authorization strategies), specific authorization models, and best practices for cloud platforms.

details: Ensures that only authorized identities have the right access to the right resources.
Acts as the new perimeter in cloud-native security, protecting sensitive resources from unauthorized access and misuse.
Gartner defines IAM as 'the security discipline that enables the right individuals to access the right resources at the right times for the right reasons.'
Requires a trust relationship, clear designation of responsibilities, and technical mechanics between Cloud Service Providers (CSPs) and Cloud Service Customers (CSCs).
IAM cannot be managed solely by the CSP or the CSC.

details.description: Restricting access to a resource, based on the permissions granted to the entity.
Verifies the identity of a user, process, or device, often as a prerequisite to allowing access to resources in a system.
The decision to permit or deny a subject access to system objects (e.g., network, data, application, service).
A mechanism through which an identity is authenticated via additional factors such as something you know, something you have or something you are.
A characteristic or property of an entity that describes its state, appearance, or other relevant aspects.
Maps identities to authorizations with required attributes (e.g., user X is allowed access to resource Y when Z attributes have designated values).
A unique, identifiable actor in a computer system (user, device, application, or system) identified and authenticated by an IAM system.
The unique expression of an entity within a given namespace.
Provides a permission-centric view, defining the access level for users to perform specific tasks.

An access control or entitlement that requires specific attributes, such as multi-factor authentication (MFA), the user logging in from a managed system, or the targeted resource having a particular tag. Preferred over RBAC.

Access requirements defined in a machine-readable policy document that typically provides extensive flexibility and granularity with support for various conditions and other variables, such as attributes. Preferred over RBAC.
A more common model where access is granted to all users with a given role (e.g., developer or administrator).

features.category: Commonly Used Standards for Cloud Computing (Authentication & Authorization Strategies)

details.mechanisms: Uses XML to make assertions (authentication statements, attribute statements, authorization decision statements) between an Identity Provider and a Relying Party.
details.usage: Widely supported by enterprise tools and CSPs; can be complex to configure initially; well-suited for traditional web-based client-server applications.

details.function: Allows users to grant third-party applications limited access to resources without sharing credentials.
details.usage: Widely used for web services (including consumer services); popular for authorizing API access or connecting 3rd parties to applications; used for delegating access control and authorizations between services.

details.function: Adds an authentication layer to OAuth.
details.mechanisms: Based on HTTP, uses URLs to identify the IdP and the user/identity.
details.usage: Very commonly seen in consumer services; growing support in commercial products (e.g., Single Page Applications like Facebook); OpenID 2.0 is deprecated and largely replaced by OIDC.

details: Develop a comprehensive policy, plan, and processes for managing cloud service identities and authorizations.
Cloud users should use MFA for all cloud access and send MFA status as an attribute when using federated authentication.
Document an entitlement matrix for each cloud deployment that aligns with security and business requirements.
Translate entitlement matrices into technical policies when supported by the CSP or platform.
Prefer Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) and Policy-Based Access Control (PBAC) over Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).
Assess and adopt more modern IAM processes and technologies such as usage tracking for improved least privilege, Just-In-Time (JIT) access, and risk scoring.
Log and monitor all IAM changes both at the Identity Provider and the Resource Provider.
Integrate plans and procedures for invalidating or restricting abused IAM session tokens into the incident response program.

references.details: Security Guidance v5.0 covers Identity and Access Management in Domain 5.
Defines IAM as 'the security discipline that enables the right individuals to access the right resources at the right times for the right reasons.'

references.source: OASIS (Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards)
